message.content: run 'onboarding' command (binary in your path)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

This host (queenbee, user dcxtc) uses a devgroup workflow that sits alongside the AgentRail saga protocol described in CLAUDE.md. Both apply.

**Branching rules:**
- `main` and `dev` are coordinator-only (mike). Never commit to or push them.
- Base new branches on `origin/dev`, NOT `origin/main`.
- Local branch naming: `feat/<slug>` for WIP, `fix/<slug>` for bug fixes, rename to `pr/<slug>` to signal "ready to merge".

  [x] 007-inline-specifier [production]: Verify inline function specifier works on function definitions (it's already ...
  [x] 008-sizeof-expr [production]: Support sizeof on expressions without parentheses: 'sizeof x' in addition to ...
  [x] 009-octal-literals [production]: Support octal integer literals in the lexer. When an integer starts with 0 an...
  [x] 010-implicit-array-size [production]: Support inferring array size from initializer: 'int a[] = {1,2,3}' infers siz...
  [x] 011-multidim-arrays [production]: Support multi-dimensional array declarations: 'int x[2][3]'. Parser: after fi...
  [>] 012-compound-literals [production]: Support compound literal syntax: (type){initializer} in expression position. ...
  [ ] 013-nested-initializers [production]: Support nested brace initializers and designated initializers. Nested braces:...
  [ ] 014-inline-asm [production]: Support asm("...") inline assembly statements. Parser: recognize 'asm' keywor...
  [ ] 015-wide-unicode-strings [production]: Support wide and unicode string/char literal prefixes: L"...", L'x', u"...", ...
  [ ] 016-struct-bitfields [production]: Support struct bitfield declarations: 'int x : 2;' in struct member position....
  [ ] 017-gcc-builtins [production]: Support __builtin_types_compatible_p(type1, type2) — a GCC builtin returnin...
  [ ] 018-fix-preprocessor-crash [production]: Fix preprocessor crash on chibicc macro.c test. Run macro.c through tc24r wit...
  [ ] 019-string-literal-concat [production]: Fix: implicit string literal concatenation ("str1" "str2" → "str1str2") is ...
  [ ] 020-stmt-expr-state-fix [production]: Fix: multiple statement expressions in same function corrupt codegen state. GH #18. When a function contains multiple GCC statement expressions ({ ... }), stack/scope state is not properly restored, causing incorrect variable lookups and offsets after the first one completes. This blocks all chibicc tests using ASSERT macros with statement expressions.
  [ ] 021-paren-declarators [production]: Support parenthesized declarators in variable declarations: char (*x)[3] (pointer to array), char (x)[3] (parenthesized declarator), char (x[3])[4]. These appear in chibicc variable.c test. Parser must handle nested parens, brackets, and stars in declarator position. GH #17.
  [ ] 022-struct-member-resolution [production]: Fix: struct member resolution uses wrong type when multiple struct types are defined. object_type in member.rs resolves against a single "current" type instead of the expression actual type, causing panics (unknown member) or wrong offsets. GH #1.
---
=== PLAN ===
# chibicc Compatibility Saga

Goal: maximize tc24r's ability to compile real C programs from three
external test suites (chibicc, beej-c-guide, bgc). Current baseline:
chibicc 6/41, beej 4/11, bgc 41/117.

Five tests are permanently out of scope (float, atomic, tls, varargs,
unicode). Of the remaining 24 actionable chibicc failures, this plan
addresses them in priority order by number of tests unlocked.

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Description                          | Tests Unlocked | Cumulative |
|-------|--------------------------------------|----------------|------------|
| 0     | Cast expressions `(type)expr`        | 5              | 11/41      |
| 1     | Stub headers (stddef.h, stdalign.h)  | 3              | 14/41      |
| 2     | goto/labels, empty for(;;)           | 2              | 16/41      |
| 3     | Compound literals, brace init        | 2              | 18/41      |
| 4     | C99/C11 keywords and specifiers      | 4              | 22/41      |
| 5     | Remaining parser features            | 5              | 27/41      |
| 6     | Test harness improvements            | —              | validate   |

---

## Phase 0: Cast Expressions

Cast expressions `(type)expr` are the single highest-value feature,
blocking 5 chibicc tests (arith, builtin, cast, pointer, sizeof).

### Step 0.1: Parse cast expressions
- Detect `(type)` prefix in expression parser (expr.rs)
- Distinguish from parenthesized expressions: `(int)x` vs `(x+1)`
- Lookahead: if token after `(` is a type keyword, parse as cast
- AST: add `Expr::Cast { ty, expr }` node
- Test: `(int)x`, `(char *)p`, `(void *)0`

### Step 0.2: Codegen for cast expressions
- Emit appropriate narrowing/widening for int↔char casts
- Pointer casts are no-ops (same size on COR24)
- Handle `(void *)0` as null pointer constant
- Test: compile and run cast demo

### Step 0.3: Add demo and verify chibicc tests
- Create demo56.c exercising cast expressions
- Re-run chibicc tests, verify arith/builtin/cast/pointer/sizeof progress
- Update testing-status.md counts

---

## Phase 1: Stub Headers

Missing freestanding headers block 3 tests (attribute, offsetof, stdhdr).

### Step 1.1: Add include/stddef.h stub
- Define NULL, size_t, ptrdiff_t
- Define offsetof() macro
- Test: `#include <stddef.h>` compiles

### Step 1.2: Add include/stdalign.h stub
- Define alignof/alignas macros (map to _Alignof/_Alignas or no-op)
- Test: `#include <stdalign.h>` compiles

### Step 1.3: Add include/stdbool.h stub
- Define bool, true, false
- Test: `#include <stdbool.h>` compiles
- Also unblocks beej-c-guide variables_and_statements.c

### Step 1.4: Verify chibicc and beej test progress
- Re-run all three test suites
- Update testing-status.md

---

## Phase 2: goto/labels and Empty For

Blocks 2 chibicc tests (control, variable) and likely several bgc examples.

### Step 2.1: Support empty clauses in for loops
- `for(;;)` — empty init, condition, increment
- `for(; i < n;)` — empty init and increment
- Parser: allow missing expressions in for statement
- Test: `for(;;) { break; }` compiles

### Step 2.2: Support goto and labels
- Parser: recognize `label:` and `goto label;`
- AST: add `Stmt::Label(name)` and `Stmt::Goto(name)`
- Codegen: emit labels and unconditional branches
- Test: demo with goto-based state machine

### Step 2.3: Verify test progress
- Re-run test suites, update docs

---

## Phase 3: Compound Literals and Brace Initializers

Blocks 2 chibicc tests (complit, initializer).

### Step 3.1: Support array brace initializers
- `int a[] = {1, 2, 3};` — infer size from initializer count
- `int a[3] = {1, 2, 3};` — brace init for arrays (not just structs)
- Parser + codegen changes

### Step 3.2: Support compound literals
- `(int){42}`, `(int[]){1, 2, 3}`, `(struct point){.x=1, .y=2}`
- Parser: `(type){init}` in expression position
- Codegen: allocate temporary, initialize, return address
- Test: demo with compound literals

### Step 3.3: Verify test progress

---

## Phase 4: C99/C11 Keywords and Specifiers

Each unlocks 1 chibicc test. 4 keywords total.

### Step 4.1: Support _Alignof / _Alignas
- Parser: recognize keywords in type/expression position
- _Alignof(type) returns alignment (always 1 on COR24 byte-addressed)
- _Alignas accepted and ignored
- Unlocks: alignof test

### Step 4.2: Support _Noreturn specifier
- Parser: accept _Noreturn before function return type
- Ignore for codegen (informational only)
- Unlocks: compat test

### Step 4.3: Support typeof operator
- Parser: `typeof(expr)` and `typeof(type)` in type position
- Evaluate type of expression at parse time
- Unlocks: typeof test

### Step 4.4: Support inline specifier on function definitions
- Parser: accept `inline` before function return type
- Ignore for codegen (no inlining)
- Unlocks: extern test

### Step 4.5: Verify test progress

---

## Phase 5: Remaining Parser Features

Each unlocks 1 chibicc test.

### Step 5.1: Struct bitfield declarations
- Parser: `int x : N` in struct member position
- Codegen: pack bitfields into words
- Unlocks: bitfield test

### Step 5.2: Extended asm syntax
- Parser: accept multi-string asm, operand constraints
- Codegen: emit string content, ignore constraints
- Unlocks: asm test

### Step 5.3: Enum/const complex initializer expressions
- Const-fold more complex expressions in enum values
- Unlocks: constexpr test

### Step 5.4: Multi-character and wide character literals
- Lexer: handle 'abcd' multi-char and L'x' wide char
- Unlocks: string, literal tests

### Step 5.5: Include path resolution for relative paths
- `#include "test/foo.c"` resolves relative to source file
- Unlocks: pragma-once test

### Step 5.6: Verify test progress and update docs

---

## Phase 6: Test Harness Improvements

### Step 6.1: Improve chibicc test awk filter
- Reduce aggressive line stripping (e.g. common_ext declarations)
- Add targeted per-test fixups where needed
- Re-evaluate all 41 tests with improved harness

### Step 6.2: Re-run beej-c-guide and bgc suites
- Test with all new features
- Update testing-status.md with final counts

### Step 6.3: Update README and docs
- Final summary table
- Feature coverage documentation

---

## Out of Scope

| Test | Reason |
|------|--------|
| float | No FPU on COR24 |
| atomic | `<stdatomic.h>` — OS-level feature |
| tls | `<pthread.h>` — OS threading |
| varargs | `<stdarg.h>` — ABI-level variadic support |
| unicode | UTF-8 identifiers — low priority for embedded |

---

## Phase 7: Bug Fixes and Parser Hardening

Addresses open GH issues not covered by the feature phases above.

### Step 7.1: Fix statement expression state corruption (GH #18)
- Multiple GCC statement expressions `({ ... })` in the same function
  corrupt codegen state (wrong stack offsets, variable lookups)
- Likely cause: StmtExpr codegen does not properly save/restore scope state
- Blocks: all chibicc tests using ASSERT macros with statement expressions

### Step 7.2: Support parenthesized declarators (GH #17)
- `char (*x)[3]` — pointer to array
- `char (x)[3]` — parenthesized declarator (equivalent to `char x[3]`)
- `char (x[3])[4]` — parenthesized array declarator
- Blocks: chibicc `variable.c` test lines 58-64

### Step 7.3: Fix struct member resolution with multiple types (GH #1)
- `object_type()` in member.rs resolves against wrong struct type when
  multiple struct types are defined in the same translation unit
- Causes panics ("unknown struct member") or wrong offsets via pointer
- Must track struct type per expression, not globally

### Step 7.4: Verify test progress and update docs
- Re-run all test suites
- Close GH issues #7, #8 (already implemented)
- Update testing-status.md

=== PLAN ===
# chibicc Compatibility Saga

Goal: maximize tc24r's ability to compile real C programs from three
external test suites (chibicc, beej-c-guide, bgc). Current baseline:
chibicc 6/41, beej 4/11, bgc 41/117.

Five tests are permanently out of scope (float, atomic, tls, varargs,
unicode). Of the remaining 24 actionable chibicc failures, this plan
addresses them in priority order by number of tests unlocked.

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Description                          | Tests Unlocked | Cumulative |
|-------|--------------------------------------|----------------|------------|
| 0     | Cast expressions `(type)expr`        | 5              | 11/41      |
| 1     | Stub headers (stddef.h, stdalign.h)  | 3              | 14/41      |
| 2     | goto/labels, empty for(;;)           | 2              | 16/41      |
| 3     | Compound literals, brace init        | 2              | 18/41      |
| 4     | C99/C11 keywords and specifiers      | 4              | 22/41      |
| 5     | Remaining parser features            | 5              | 27/41      |
| 6     | Test harness improvements            | —              | validate   |

---

## Phase 0: Cast Expressions

Cast expressions `(type)expr` are the single highest-value feature,
blocking 5 chibicc tests (arith, builtin, cast, pointer, sizeof).

### Step 0.1: Parse cast expressions
- Detect `(type)` prefix in expression parser (expr.rs)
- Distinguish from parenthesized expressions: `(int)x` vs `(x+1)`
- Lookahead: if token after `(` is a type keyword, parse as cast
- AST: add `Expr::Cast { ty, expr }` node
- Test: `(int)x`, `(char *)p`, `(void *)0`

### Step 0.2: Codegen for cast expressions
- Emit appropriate narrowing/widening for int↔char casts
- Pointer casts are no-ops (same size on COR24)
- Handle `(void *)0` as null pointer constant
- Test: compile and run cast demo

### Step 0.3: Add demo and verify chibicc tests
- Create demo56.c exercising cast expressions
- Re-run chibicc tests, verify arith/builtin/cast/pointer/sizeof progress
- Update testing-status.md counts

---

## Phase 1: Stub Headers

Missing freestanding headers block 3 tests (attribute, offsetof, stdhdr).

### Step 1.1: Add include/stddef.h stub
- Define NULL, size_t, ptrdiff_t
- Define offsetof() macro
- Test: `#include <stddef.h>` compiles

### Step 1.2: Add include/stdalign.h stub
- Define alignof/alignas macros (map to _Alignof/_Alignas or no-op)
- Test: `#include <stdalign.h>` compiles

### Step 1.3: Add include/stdbool.h stub
- Define bool, true, false
- Test: `#include <stdbool.h>` compiles
- Also unblocks beej-c-guide variables_and_statements.c

### Step 1.4: Verify chibicc and beej test progress
- Re-run all three test suites
- Update testing-status.md

---

## Phase 2: goto/labels and Empty For

Blocks 2 chibicc tests (control, variable) and likely several bgc examples.

### Step 2.1: Support empty clauses in for loops
- `for(;;)` — empty init, condition, increment
- `for(; i < n;)` — empty init and increment
- Parser: allow missing expressions in for statement
- Test: `for(;;) { break; }` compiles

### Step 2.2: Support goto and labels
- Parser: recognize `label:` and `goto label;`
- AST: add `Stmt::Label(name)` and `Stmt::Goto(name)`
- Codegen: emit labels and unconditional branches
- Test: demo with goto-based state machine

### Step 2.3: Verify test progress
- Re-run test suites, update docs

---

## Phase 3: Compound Literals and Brace Initializers

Blocks 2 chibicc tests (complit, initializer).

### Step 3.1: Support array brace initializers
- `int a[] = {1, 2, 3};` — infer size from initializer count
- `int a[3] = {1, 2, 3};` — brace init for arrays (not just structs)
- Parser + codegen changes

### Step 3.2: Support compound literals
- `(int){42}`, `(int[]){1, 2, 3}`, `(struct point){.x=1, .y=2}`
- Parser: `(type){init}` in expression position
- Codegen: allocate temporary, initialize, return address
- Test: demo with compound literals

### Step 3.3: Verify test progress

---

## Phase 4: C99/C11 Keywords and Specifiers

Each unlocks 1 chibicc test. 4 keywords total.

### Step 4.1: Support _Alignof / _Alignas
- Parser: recognize keywords in type/expression position
- _Alignof(type) returns alignment (always 1 on COR24 byte-addressed)
- _Alignas accepted and ignored
- Unlocks: alignof test

### Step 4.2: Support _Noreturn specifier
- Parser: accept _Noreturn before function return type
- Ignore for codegen (informational only)
- Unlocks: compat test

### Step 4.3: Support typeof operator
- Parser: `typeof(expr)` and `typeof(type)` in type position
- Evaluate type of expression at parse time
- Unlocks: typeof test

### Step 4.4: Support inline specifier on function definitions
- Parser: accept `inline` before function return type
- Ignore for codegen (no inlining)
- Unlocks: extern test

### Step 4.5: Verify test progress

---

## Phase 5: Remaining Parser Features

Each unlocks 1 chibicc test.

### Step 5.1: Struct bitfield declarations
- Parser: `int x : N` in struct member position
- Codegen: pack bitfields into words
- Unlocks: bitfield test

### Step 5.2: Extended asm syntax
- Parser: accept multi-string asm, operand constraints
- Codegen: emit string content, ignore constraints
- Unlocks: asm test

### Step 5.3: Enum/const complex initializer expressions
- Const-fold more complex expressions in enum values
- Unlocks: constexpr test

### Step 5.4: Multi-character and wide character literals
- Lexer: handle 'abcd' multi-char and L'x' wide char
- Unlocks: string, literal tests

### Step 5.5: Include path resolution for relative paths
- `#include "test/foo.c"` resolves relative to source file
- Unlocks: pragma-once test

### Step 5.6: Verify test progress and update docs

---

## Phase 6: Test Harness Improvements

### Step 6.1: Improve chibicc test awk filter
- Reduce aggressive line stripping (e.g. common_ext declarations)
- Add targeted per-test fixups where needed
- Re-evaluate all 41 tests with improved harness

### Step 6.2: Re-run beej-c-guide and bgc suites
- Test with all new features
- Update testing-status.md with final counts

### Step 6.3: Update README and docs
- Final summary table
- Feature coverage documentation

---

|------|--------|

| atomic | `<stdatomic.h>` — OS-level feature |
| tls | `<pthread.h>` — OS threading |
| varargs | `<stdarg.h>` — ABI-level variadic support |
| unicode | UTF-8 identifiers — low priority for embedded |

## Phase 7: Bug Fixes and Parser Hardening

Addresses open GH issues not covered by the feature phases above.

### Step 7.1: Fix statement expression state corruption (GH #18)
- Multiple GCC statement expressions `({ ... })` in the same function
  corrupt codegen state (wrong stack offsets, variable lookups)
- Likely cause: StmtExpr codegen does not properly save/restore scope state
- Blocks: all chibicc tests using ASSERT macros with statement expressions

### Step 7.2: Support parenthesized declarators (GH #17)
- `char (*x)[3]` — pointer to array
- `char (x)[3]` — parenthesized declarator (equivalent to `char x[3]`)
- `char (x[3])[4]` — parenthesized array declarator
- Blocks: chibicc `variable.c` test lines 58-64

### Step 7.3: Fix struct member resolution with multiple types (GH #1)
- `object_type()` in member.rs resolves against wrong struct type when
  multiple struct types are defined in the same translation unit
- Causes panics ("unknown struct member") or wrong offsets via pointer
- Must track struct type per expression, not globally

### Step 7.4: Verify test progress and update docs
- Re-run all test suites
- Close GH issues #7, #8 (already implemented)
- Update testing-status.md

Workflow CLI for keeping AI agents on track

Usage: agentrail [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>

Commands:
  init            Initialize a new saga
  setup           Bootstrap a project: create saga, CLAUDE.md, and register domain
  add             Add a step to the saga (for maintenance mode or ad-hoc tasks)
  status          Show current saga state
  next            Output current step prompt and context for a fresh agent session
  begin           Mark the current step as in-progress
  complete        Complete current step, optionally define next step
  plan            View or update the saga plan

  distill         Distill trajectories into a skill document
  run-loop        Auto-execute deterministic steps, pause at agent steps
  abort           Mark current step as blocked

input.command: agentrail insert --help 2>&1

content.content:
Insert a new pending step at a chosen position, shifting later steps up

Use when a bug or unplanned task needs to slot in before existing pending work. The new step lands at position `after + 1`; every pending/in-progress step with a number greater than `after` shifts up by one (step.toml `number` and the on-disk `NNN-slug` directory name are both updated). The operation is refused if any step that would shift is already `Completed`, since completed steps anchor git-tracked history.

Pass `--after 0` to prepend at position 1 (only works if step 1 is pending).

Cursor semantic (preemption): if the new step lands at or before the current cursor, focus follows the new arrival — the inserted step becomes `current_step` and the previously focused step is pushed to `current_step + 1`. This matches the "drop in a blocker" use case. If the new step lands behind the cursor, focus is unchanged and the new step is queued as future work.

Usage: agentrail insert [OPTIONS] --after <AFTER> --slug <SLUG> --prompt <PROMPT>

Options:
      --after <AFTER>
          Position to insert after (0 prepends). The new step becomes N+1

      --slug <SLUG>
          Slug for the new step

      --prompt <PROMPT>
          Step prompt: text, file path, or "-" for stdin

      --role <ROLE>
          Step role (production, deterministic, validation, meta)
          
          [default: production]

      --task-type <TASK_TYPE>
          Task type for skill/trajectory lookup

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

content.content:
1	# Brief: support const-expr in array sizes in `tc24r`
2	
3	**Owner:** dcxtc
4	**Branch:** `pr/array-size-expressions`
5	**Repo:** `sw-cor24-x-tinyc`
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	`tc24r` is the C-to-COR24 cross-transpiler. It currently parses array sizes as a **single token only** — a literal or `#define`'d constant. Anything beyond that errors.
10	
11	Concrete failure observed in `sw-cor24-plsw/src/macro.h:387`:
12	```c
13	char  mac_arg_buf[MAC_ARG_MAX * MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX];
14	/* tc24r: error at offset 15032: expected RBracket, got Star */
15	```
16	
17	Minimal repro:
18	```c
19	#define A 8
20	#define B 16
21	char buf[A * B];        /* fails: expected RBracket, got Star */
22	char buf2[BUF_SIZE];    /* OK */
23	```
24	
25	Constant-expression array sizes are normal C (have been since C89). dcpls is shipping a stopgap `#define`-the-product workaround in PL/SW to unblock today, but the proper fix is here in `tc24r`.
26	
27	## Goal
28	
29	Extend `tc24r`'s array-size grammar to accept compile-time-evaluable integer expressions. After this saga, the failing repro above parses cleanly and `tc24r` emits the same machine code it would for the equivalent literal-sized array.
30	
31	## Scope
32	
33	Support these inside `[ ... ]` for array sizes:
34	
35	| Construct | Example | Required |
36	|---|---|---|
37	| Integer literal | `[42]`, `[0x100]` | already works |
38	| Single identifier (constant) | `[BUF_SIZE]` | already works |
39	| Binary `*`, `+`, `-`, `/` | `[A * B]`, `[N + 1]`, `[N - 1]`, `[N / 2]` | **add** |
40	| Parens for grouping | `[(A + B) * C]`, `[A * (B + C)]` | **add** |
41	| Mix of literals and identifiers | `[N * 16]`, `[256 + N]` | **add** |
42	| Unary `-` | `[-1]` (probably nonsense in array context but parser-level OK) | nice-to-have |
43	| `sizeof(...)` | `[sizeof(int)]`, `[sizeof(struct X)]` | **out of scope** for this PR — separate concern |
44	| Function calls or runtime values | `[strlen(s)]` | out of scope (would be VLA — also out of scope) |
45	
46	**Operator precedence:** standard C — `*` and `/` higher than `+` and `-`. Left-associative. Parens override. Match what a C99-conforming compiler would do for the same expression.
47	
48	**Evaluation:** at parse/codegen time, fold the expression to a single integer constant. Reject (with a clear error) anything that can't be reduced to a compile-time integer (e.g. references to non-`#define`'d identifiers).
49	
50	**Errors:** if an identifier inside the expression isn't a known compile-time constant, error with a message that names the offending identifier and clearly says "must be a compile-time integer constant."
51	
52	## Suggested implementation approach (don't have to follow this)
53	
54	If `tc24r`'s parser already has a general expression parser somewhere (e.g., for initializers like `int x = A * B;`), reuse it for array sizes — they should share the same const-expr machinery. If today the array-size path has its own one-token parser, replace it with a call into the general expression parser, gated to compile-time-evaluable nodes.
55	
56	If `tc24r` has no general expression evaluator yet, this saga doesn't have to ship one — minimal const-expr parser supporting the operators above is enough.
57	
58	## Tests
59	
60	In `tests/` (or wherever your fixture pattern lives):
61	
62	1. **Positive cases** — verify these produce the same output as their literal-sized equivalents:
63	   - `char buf[A * B];` where `A=8, B=16` should match `char buf[128];`
64	   - `int xs[N + 1];`
65	   - `char grid[ROWS * COLS];`
66	   - `char nested[(A + B) * 2];` (parens)
67	   - Mixed: `char cell[N * 16];` (identifier × literal)
68	
69	2. **Negative cases**:
70	   - `int xs[i * 2];` where `i` is a runtime variable — error mentioning that `i` must be compile-time-constant.
71	   - `char ar[];` (incomplete array — should error or behave as today)
72	   - `char ar[A *];` (broken expression) — clear parse error.
73	
74	3. **Regression**: existing fixtures with literal sizes and single-identifier sizes still pass.
75	
76	## Migration
77	
78	Once this lands and mike installs the new `tc24r` to `work/bin/`:
79	
80	- dcpls can revert their `MAC_ARG_BUF_SIZE` workaround in `macro.h` and restore the cleaner `char mac_arg_buf[MAC_ARG_MAX * MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX]` form. That's a follow-up dcpls saga, not yours.
81	- Other repos may have similar workarounds (or `[A * B]` lines that haven't been hit because the file wasn't compiled yet); a project-wide grep follow-up is worth scheduling.
82	
83	## What goes in this PR
84	
85	1. Extend the array-size parser to support const-expr with `* / + -` and parens.
86	2. Compile-time integer evaluation that reduces these expressions to a single number.
87	3. Tests above.
88	4. Update `README.md` (or relevant docs) noting the supported expression forms.
89	
90	## What does NOT go in this PR
91	
92	- No `sizeof(...)` support — separate saga.
93	- No VLA or runtime-sized arrays.
94	- No general expression parser overhaul if `tc24r` doesn't have one — minimal const-expr only.
95	- No changes to `sw-cor24-plsw` — that's dcpls's stopgap saga and dcpls's later cleanup, not yours.
96	
97	## When done
98	
99	Push `pr/array-size-expressions` and signal. After relay, mike rebuilds and reinstalls `tc24r` to `work/bin/`. Existing PL/SW workarounds become redundant (dcpls will clean those up separately). Other downstream repos that may have hit similar issues unblock automatically.
100

file.content:
# Brief: support const-expr in array sizes in `tc24r`

**Owner:** dcxtc
**Branch:** `pr/array-size-expressions`
**Repo:** `sw-cor24-x-tinyc`

## Context

`tc24r` is the C-to-COR24 cross-transpiler. It currently parses array sizes as a **single token only** — a literal or `#define`'d constant. Anything beyond that errors.

Concrete failure observed in `sw-cor24-plsw/src/macro.h:387`:
```c
char  mac_arg_buf[MAC_ARG_MAX * MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX];
/* tc24r: error at offset 15032: expected RBracket, got Star */
```

Minimal repro:
```c
#define A 8
#define B 16
char buf[A * B];        /* fails: expected RBracket, got Star */
char buf2[BUF_SIZE];    /* OK */
```

Constant-expression array sizes are normal C (have been since C89). dcpls is shipping a stopgap `#define`-the-product workaround in PL/SW to unblock today, but the proper fix is here in `tc24r`.

## Goal

Extend `tc24r`'s array-size grammar to accept compile-time-evaluable integer expressions. After this saga, the failing repro above parses cleanly and `tc24r` emits the same machine code it would for the equivalent literal-sized array.

## Scope

Support these inside `[ ... ]` for array sizes:

| Construct | Example | Required |
|---|---|---|
| Integer literal | `[42]`, `[0x100]` | already works |
| Single identifier (constant) | `[BUF_SIZE]` | already works |
| Binary `*`, `+`, `-`, `/` | `[A * B]`, `[N + 1]`, `[N - 1]`, `[N / 2]` | **add** |
| Parens for grouping | `[(A + B) * C]`, `[A * (B + C)]` | **add** |
| Mix of literals and identifiers | `[N * 16]`, `[256 + N]` | **add** |
| Unary `-` | `[-1]` (probably nonsense in array context but parser-level OK) | nice-to-have |
| `sizeof(...)` | `[sizeof(int)]`, `[sizeof(struct X)]` | **out of scope** for this PR — separate concern |
| Function calls or runtime values | `[strlen(s)]` | out of scope (would be VLA — also out of scope) |

**Operator precedence:** standard C — `*` and `/` higher than `+` and `-`. Left-associative. Parens override. Match what a C99-conforming compiler would do for the same expression.

**Evaluation:** at parse/codegen time, fold the expression to a single integer constant. Reject (with a clear error) anything that can't be reduced to a compile-time integer (e.g. references to non-`#define`'d identifiers).

**Errors:** if an identifier inside the expression isn't a known compile-time constant, error with a message that names the offending identifier and clearly says "must be a compile-time integer constant."

## Suggested implementation approach (don't have to follow this)

If `tc24r`'s parser already has a general expression parser somewhere (e.g., for initializers like `int x = A * B;`), reuse it for array sizes — they should share the same const-expr machinery. If today the array-size path has its own one-token parser, replace it with a call into the general expression parser, gated to compile-time-evaluable nodes.

If `tc24r` has no general expression evaluator yet, this saga doesn't have to ship one — minimal const-expr parser supporting the operators above is enough.

## Tests

In `tests/` (or wherever your fixture pattern lives):

1. **Positive cases** — verify these produce the same output as their literal-sized equivalents:
   - `char buf[A * B];` where `A=8, B=16` should match `char buf[128];`
   - `int xs[N + 1];`
   - `char grid[ROWS * COLS];`
   - `char nested[(A + B) * 2];` (parens)
   - Mixed: `char cell[N * 16];` (identifier × literal)

2. **Negative cases**:
   - `int xs[i * 2];` where `i` is a runtime variable — error mentioning that `i` must be compile-time-constant.
   - `char ar[];` (incomplete array — should error or behave as today)
   - `char ar[A *];` (broken expression) — clear parse error.

3. **Regression**: existing fixtures with literal sizes and single-identifier sizes still pass.

## Migration

Once this lands and mike installs the new `tc24r` to `work/bin/`:

- dcpls can revert their `MAC_ARG_BUF_SIZE` workaround in `macro.h` and restore the cleaner `char mac_arg_buf[MAC_ARG_MAX * MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX]` form. That's a follow-up dcpls saga, not yours.
- Other repos may have similar workarounds (or `[A * B]` lines that haven't been hit because the file wasn't compiled yet); a project-wide grep follow-up is worth scheduling.

## What goes in this PR

1. Extend the array-size parser to support const-expr with `* / + -` and parens.
2. Compile-time integer evaluation that reduces these expressions to a single number.
3. Tests above.
4. Update `README.md` (or relevant docs) noting the supported expression forms.

## What does NOT go in this PR

- No `sizeof(...)` support — separate saga.
- No VLA or runtime-sized arrays.
- No general expression parser overhaul if `tc24r` doesn't have one — minimal const-expr only.
- No changes to `sw-cor24-plsw` — that's dcpls's stopgap saga and dcpls's later cleanup, not yours.

## When done

Push `pr/array-size-expressions` and signal. After relay, mike rebuilds and reinstalls `tc24r` to `work/bin/`. Existing PL/SW workarounds become redundant (dcpls will clean those up separately). Other downstream repos that may have hit similar issues unblock automatically.

input.command: agentrail insert --after 11 --slug array-size-expressions --task-type rust-clippy-fix --prompt "Extend tc24r's array-size grammar to accept compile-time integer constant expressions inside [ ... ]. Currently only a single literal or #define-resolved identifier is accepted (e.g. [BUF_SIZE] works, [A*B] fails with 'expected RBracket, got Star'). Support binary * / + - with standard C precedence (left-associative, * / higher than + -), unary -, and parens for grouping. Identifiers are resolved through #define lookup; non-constant identifiers must error with a clear message naming the offender ('must be a compile-time integer constant'). Constant-fold to a single integer at parse time so codegen matches the literal-sized equivalent. Out of scope: sizeof, function calls, VLAs. Add positive tests (A*B, N+1, (A+B)*C, N*16, A*(B+C)) verifying codegen matches literal-sized equivalents, and negative tests (runtime variable, broken expression A*). Update README.md to note supported forms. See full brief: /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/dcxtc-array-size-expressions.md"

  [ ] 021-stmt-expr-state-fix [production]: Fix: multiple statement expressions in same function corrupt codegen state. GH #18. When a function contains multiple GCC statement expressions ({ ... }), stack/scope state is not properly restored, causing incorrect variable lookups and offsets after the first one completes. This blocks all chibicc tests using ASSERT macros with statement expressions.
  [ ] 022-paren-declarators [production]: Support parenthesized declarators in variable declarations: char (*x)[3] (pointer to array), char (x)[3] (parenthesized declarator), char (x[3])[4].   [ ] 023-struct-member-resolution [production]: Fix: struct member resolution uses wrong type when multiple struct types are defined. object_type in member.rs resolves against a single "current" type instead of the expression actual type, causing panics (unknown member) or wrong offsets. GH #1.

input.description: Locate array-size parser and const-expr evaluator
input.prompt:
I'm looking at a Rust C-compiler codebase at /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/dcxtc/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-tinyc. I need to find specific things related to how array sizes inside `[ ... ]` are parsed, and whether any const-expression evaluator already exists.

The known failure is: `char buf[A * B]` errors with "expected RBracket, got Star" — so currently the array-size parser only accepts a single token (literal or identifier).

Please find and report (with file:line references):

1. **Where array-size brackets are parsed.** The parser seems split across `components/frontend/crates/tc24r-parser/src/{decl.rs,stmt.rs,expr.rs}`, `tc24r-parser-types/src/{type_parse.rs,type_detect.rs}`, etc. Find the function(s) that handle `[size]` after a declarator name. Look for something that consumes `LBracket` then expects an integer/identifier then `RBracket`. Likely in decl.rs (variable declarations), type_parse.rs, or a "declarator" function. Show me the exact code that parses the bracket expression and report what kinds of tokens it accepts today.

2. **How identifiers in array sizes are resolved to integers.** Does the preprocessor substitute `#define`'d identifiers into literal tokens before the parser sees them? (Look at tc24r-preprocess crate). Or does the parser look up the value at parse time? This determines whether I need a symbol table or can rely on already-substituted literals.

3. **Existing const-expr evaluator, if any.** Search broadly:
   - Anywhere that const-folds: `const_fold`, `eval_const`, `evaluate`, `to_const`, `fold`, `compile_time`
   - Enum value parsing in `tc24r-parser-enum/src/enum_parse.rs` — enums often need const-expr for initializers
   - Initializers for global/static variables in decl.rs
   - sizeof handling in expr.rs (sizeof(int) needs to evaluate to a number)
   - Any `bitwise.rs` / `arithmetic.rs` infrastructure that handles `*`, `+`, `-`, `/` at parse time vs codegen time
   Report what exists and how complete it is for the operators `* / + -` plus parens.

4. **Lexer token names** for `*`, `/`, `+`, `-`, `(`, `)`, `[`, `]`. Show me the relevant enum from `tc24r-lexer/src/lib.rs` or wherever the Token type is defined.

5. **Existing tests for array declarations** — patterns of test fixtures, where they live (`tests/`, `work/reg-rs/`, `demos/`). What's the test idiom? Are there tests for `[N]` with `#define N 10`?

Report each finding succinctly with file:line references and short code excerpts. I need enough to design the implementation but don't read everything — just enough to answer the five questions above. Under 600 words.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me compile my findings:

## Summary: Array Size Parser Investigation

### 1. **Array-size bracket parsing location**
**File:** `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/dcxtc/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-tinyc/components/frontend/crates/tc24r-parser/src/decl.rs` (lines 16–79)

The array size is parsed by `parse_const_array_size()` which calls a recursive descent expression evaluator:
- `parse_const_array_size()` → `const_add_sub()` → `const_mul_div()` → `const_primary()`
- Lines 118–120, 278–285, 370–372: Callers consume `LBracket`, call `parse_const_array_size()`, then expect `RBracket`
- Currently `const_primary()` (lines 62–79) only accepts `IntLit(v)`, unary minus, or parenthesized expressions—**no identifier support**
- Error "expected integer constant in array size" at line 76

**Current limitation:** `const_primary()` does NOT handle `TokenKind::Ident(_)`, which is why `char buf[A * B]` fails at the `Star` token (line 44 in `const_mul_div()` tries to parse the `*` as a primary, fails).

### 2. **Identifier resolution in array sizes**
**File:** `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/dcxtc/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-tinyc/components/frontend/crates/tc24r-preprocess/src/preprocess.rs` (lines 119–140)

The preprocessor **substitutes `#define`'d identifiers into literal tokens** before the parser sees them:
- Line 137: `substitute::expand_line(line, &ctx.defines, &ctx.func_macros)` replaces all macro/define text
- Line 119–120: `#define` stores the expansion string in `ctx.defines: HashMap<String, String>`
- This means preprocessor-defined array sizes are already literal integers when the parser receives tokens

**Design implication:** You need a symbol table to resolve enum constants and `#define`'d identifiers at parse time; the preprocessor already handles simple text substitution but doesn't affect the parser's `const_primary()` function.

### 3. **Existing const-expr evaluator**
**YES, partially complete:**
- **Const expression evaluator:** `decl.rs` lines 16–79 implements `const_add_sub()`, `const_mul_div()`, `const_primary()` for `*`, `/`, `+`, `-`, parens, and unary minus
- **Enum value parsing:** `/components/frontend/crates/tc24r-parser-enum/src/enum_parse.rs` (lines 42–52) evaluates enum initializers (only integer literals and unary minus, no expressions)
- **sizeof handling:** `/components/frontend/crates/tc24r-parser/src/expr.rs` (lines 264–272) only accepts `IntLit` in array suffixes (same single-token limitation)
- **Arithmetic operators:** `arithmetic.rs` and `bitwise.rs` exist but are for runtime expression parsing, not const-folding

**What exists:** Operator precedence and evaluation for `* / + -` and parens. **What's missing:** Identifier lookup (enums, defines).

### 4. **Lexer token names**
**File:** `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/dcxtc/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-tinyc/components/core/crates/tc24r-token/src/kind.rs`

Token enum variants relevant to array-size expressions:
- `LParen`, `RParen` (lines 53–54)
- `LBracket`, `RBracket` (lines 57–58)
- `Star`, `Slash` (lines 69–70)
- `Plus`, `Minus` (implicit from grep results)
- `IntLit(v)` (variant with integer payload)

### 5. **Existing tests for array declarations**
**Location:** `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/dcxtc/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-tinyc/components/frontend/crates/tc24r-parser-tests/src/tests_globals.rs`

Test patterns:
- Lines 10–16: String initialization with inferred size `char msg[] = "hello"` → inferred to 6
- Lines 19–26: Init list with inferred size `int msg[] = {72, 105, 0}` → inferred to 3
- Lines 29–37: Explicit size `int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30}` → fixed to 5
- All tests use **literal integer sizes** (no `#define` or expressions)
- Test idiom: `parse_source(code)` returns AST, assertions check `Type::Array(elem, N)`

**No tests exist for `#define`'d or expression array sizes.** This is your implementation target.

---

**Next steps:** Extend `const_primary()` to accept identifiers, add a symbol-table lookup (enum constants + defines), and you'll unblock `char buf[A * B]`.

content.subject: Insert new agentrail step for array-size-expressions
content.description: Use `agentrail insert --after 11` to slot array-size-expressions ahead of compound-literals (012). Step preempts cursor.

content.subject: Bootstrap feat/array-size-expressions branch
content.description: Create local dev (origin/dev doesn't exist yet), then create feat/array-size-expressions from it.

content.subject: Investigate current array-size parser in tc24r
content.description: Find where array bracket sizes are parsed; locate any existing const-expr evaluator (e.g. for enum values, sizeof, initializers); decide reuse vs minimal const-expr parser.

content.subject: Implement const-expr support in array-size parser
content.description: Support `*`, `/`, `+`, `-`, parens, unary `-`, integer literals, and `#define`-resolved identifiers. Standard C precedence. Constant-fold to a single integer at parse time. Reject runtime/non-const identifiers with clear error naming the offender.

content.subject: Add positive and negative tests

content.text:
**Key finding to surface before going further.** This work appears to already be shipped:

- Commit `c641f9d` (Mike Wright, 2026-04-01) added `parse_const_array_size` with `+ - * /`, parens, unary minus — applied to all 4 array-size parse sites (globals, struct members, locals, function-pointer arrays).
- `demos/demo55.c` already exercises exactly the brief's patterns: `int buf[ROWS * COLS]`, `char tag[2 + 3]`, `int local[ROWS + COLS]`, `#define TOTAL (ROWS * COLS)`.
- Brief is dated today (2026-05-07), commit was 5 weeks ago.

So the brief is either stale, OR the PL/SW failure (`[MAC_ARG_MAX * MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX]`) is hitting a specific case `c641f9d` misses — most likely candidates:
1. `MAC_ARG_MAX` / `MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX` are enum constants (not `#define`s) — preprocessor wouldn't substitute them, parser sees `Ident Star Ident`, `const_primary` rejects `Ident`.
2. They're `#define`'d in a header tc24r isn't including (path/conditional issue).
3. Some bracket position the c641f9d patch missed (e.g., typedef-with-array, cast-with-array).

Before I write any code, I need to know what `MAC_ARG_MAX` actually is in PL/SW, and whether the `expected RBracket, got Star` error reproduces today against current `tc24r`. The brief reads as if written without checking that c641f9d already exists.

dcpls says:
Answer for dcxtc on the macro.h declarations:

  MAC_ARG_MAX and MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX are plain #defines — vanilla integer-literal preprocessor macros, defined in the same file
  (src/macro.h) immediately above the failing array decl:

  /* src/macro.h:382-388 */
  #define MAC_ARG_MAX     8
  #define MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX 64
  #define MAC_EXPAND_MAX  512

  /* Argument values during expansion (parallel to clauses) */
  char  mac_arg_buf[MAC_ARG_MAX * MAC_ARG_VAL_MAX];
  int   mac_arg_set[MAC_ARG_MAX];

  They are NOT enum constants. They're not in another header. tc24r src/main.c -I src includes src/macro.h directly. The error is tc24r:
   error at offset 165286: expected RBracket, got Star (this offset is in main.c after preprocessor expansion of macro.h — the actual
  offending line is macro.h:387).

  tc24r binary: /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/bin/tc24r, dated May 3 2026, mike-installed, no version flag (tc24r with no
   args just prints usage).

  My recommendation to dcxtc: option 2. The brief isn't stale — the failure is real and reproducing today against the binary on PATH. If
   c641f9d is supposed to handle this, then either (a) c641f9d isn't in the May-3 binary, (b) there's a regression on top of it, or (c)
  the patch missed the specific shape [<DEFINE> * <DEFINE>] (two #define-substituted identifiers, both of which evaluate to int
  literals). The minimal repro for the brief was char buf[A * B] with #define A 8 / #define B 16 — that's exactly what PL/SW has.
  Running it against current tc24r will tell you whether the gap is pre-c641f9d or post-c641f9d.

content.text:
**Diagnosis sharpens significantly.** The May-3 binary fails on the bare-literal case `char buf[8 * 16];` — no macros, no defines, no preprocessor involvement. Two integer literals separated by a star, and the parser still rejects the star.

Yet the current source (HEAD) clearly has the cascade in `decl.rs:16-79` and globals at `decl.rs:284` route through it. Source and binary disagree. Two plausible explanations:

1. **The May-3 binary was built from a tree without `c641f9d`'s cascade.** Possible if the build was done in a different worktree, or if `cargo` cached a stale artifact, or if the install script picked up wrong sources. Building from current source here would make `[8*16]` work and the saga is mostly already done — I just need to add identifier support to `const_primary` if needed and add tests.

2. **There's a second bug somewhere in the path that the cascade lives in but doesn't dominate.** E.g., the global-decl path may have a different LBracket consumer in some branch I haven't read. Less likely given line 284 looks clean, but possible.

To distinguish, I need to build from current source and run that binary against the same repro. That requires the emulator dep.

You said "stand by" on the emulator earlier and I've held off. I'm now blocked on it to make any further progress. Can I clone `/disk1/.../bare/sw-cor24-emulator.git` into `../sw-cor24-emulator` (the path the Cargo manifest expects)? It's a non-destructive setup step matching the standard devgroup layout — easy to undo with `rm -rf` if you had a different plan.

Two things to surface before continuing:

**Diagnostic, finalized:** Locally-built `tc24r` from current source compiles `[8*16]` and `[A*B]` cleanly — emits a correct 128-byte `.data` block. Build succeeds against `sw-cor24-isa`. Now I need to think hard about what this saga's actual scope reduces to, given the parser work is already in HEAD source.

What's left vs the brief, after the diagnostic:
1. **Parser implementation**: ✅ already done by `c641f9d`
2. **`#define`-resolved identifiers**: ✅ work today via preprocessor text substitution (just confirmed `[A*B]` with two `#define`s compiles)
3. **Tests**: brief asks for positive/negative coverage. demo55 covers positives but nothing in `work/reg-rs/` locks it in as a regression test, and there are no negative-case fixtures. Per CLAUDE.md's "Regression Test Policy," missing reg-rs coverage is the real gap.
4. **README docs**: brief asks for a note on supported expression forms. Probably not present.
5. **Cargo.toml path migration**: now folded in.
6. **Error message quality**: brief asks negative cases to name the offending identifier. Current message is `"expected integer constant in array size"` — doesn't name it. Minor improvement, optional.

So the saga collapses to: add reg-rs regression tests, README note, fold the Cargo.toml change, push as `pr/array-size-expressions`. Mike rebuilds → reinstalls → PL/SW unblocks.

content.content:
demos/demo55.c
demos/run-demo55.sh
---
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
ROOT_DIR="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
CC24="$ROOT_DIR/components/cli/target/release/tc24r"
INCLUDE_DIR="$ROOT_DIR/include"
SRC="$SCRIPT_DIR/demo55.c"
cargo build --manifest-path "$ROOT_DIR/components/cli/Cargo.toml" --release --quiet
TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d)
trap 'rm -rf "$TMPDIR"' EXIT
"$CC24" "$SRC" -o "$TMPDIR/demo55.s" -I "$INCLUDE_DIR"
echo "=== Compiled demo55.c ==="
OUTPUT=$(cor24-run --run "$TMPDIR/demo55.s" --dump --speed 0 --time 10 2>&1)
R0=$(echo "$OUTPUT" | grep "r0:" | head -1 | awk -F'[()]' '{print $2}' | tr -d ' ')
HALTED=$(echo "$OUTPUT" | grep "Halted:" | head -1 | awk '{print $2}')
if [ "$HALTED" = "true" ] && [ "$R0" = "55" ]; then
    echo "Demo 55 PASSED (r0=55)"
else
    echo "Demo 55 FAILED (r0=$R0, halted=$HALTED)"
    exit 1
fi

14	- All standard C operators with correct precedence
15	- Control flow: if/else, while, do...while, for, break, continue, switch/case

17	- Prefix/postfix increment and decrement (++i, i--)
18	- Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>=
19	- sizeof, typedef, enum, struct (dot and arrow access), union

22	- Function prototypes (forward declarations, mutual recursion)
23	- Functions with multiple parameters, recursion, ISR support
24	- Globals, string constants, hex literals
25	- Pointer arithmetic with element-size scaling
26	- Preprocessor: #define, #include, #pragma once
27	- Inline assembly: asm("...")
28	- MMIO: LED, UART TX/RX, interrupt enable
29	- Software division and modulo runtime
30

32	
33	- Multi-file compilation

37	
38	- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
39	- **8 registers**: r0-r2 (general purpose), fp, sp, z (zero), iv, ir

file.content:
### What Works

- Types: int (24-bit), unsigned int, char (8-bit), void, pointers, arrays
- All standard C operators with correct precedence
- Control flow: if/else, while, do...while, for, break, continue, switch/case
- Ternary operator (? :), character literals, multi-declaration
- Prefix/postfix increment and decrement (++i, i--)
- Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>=
- sizeof, typedef, enum, struct (dot and arrow access), union
- Function pointers: local/global declarations, arrays, parameter passing, indirect calls
- Constant expressions in array sizes (int buf[ROWS * COLS])
- Function prototypes (forward declarations, mutual recursion)
- Functions with multiple parameters, recursion, ISR support
- Globals, string constants, hex literals
- Pointer arithmetic with element-size scaling
- Preprocessor: #define, #include, #pragma once
- Inline assembly: asm("...")
- MMIO: LED, UART TX/RX, interrupt enable
- Software division and modulo runtime

### What Does Not Work Yet

- Multi-file compilation
- float/double (COR24 has no FPU -- out of scope)

## COR24 Architecture at a Glance

- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
- **8 registers**: r0-r2 (general purpose), fp, sp, z (zero), iv, ir

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Demo 62: parenthesised and mixed const-expr in array sizes
set -euo pipefail
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
ROOT_DIR="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
CC24="$ROOT_DIR/components/cli/target/release/tc24r"
INCLUDE_DIR="$ROOT_DIR/include"
SRC="$SCRIPT_DIR/demo62.c"
cargo build --manifest-path "$ROOT_DIR/components/cli/Cargo.toml" --release --quiet
TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d)
trap 'rm -rf "$TMPDIR"' EXIT
"$CC24" "$SRC" -o "$TMPDIR/demo62.s" -I "$INCLUDE_DIR"
echo "=== Compiled demo62.c ==="
OUTPUT=$(cor24-run --run "$TMPDIR/demo62.s" --dump --speed 0 --time 10 2>&1)
R0=$(echo "$OUTPUT" | grep "r0:" | head -1 | awk -F'[()]' '{print $2}' | tr -d ' ')
HALTED=$(echo "$OUTPUT" | grep "Halted:" | head -1 | awk '{print $2}')
if [ "$HALTED" = "true" ] && [ "$R0" = "62" ]; then
    echo "Demo 62 PASSED (r0=62)"
else
    echo "Demo 62 FAILED (r0=$R0, halted=$HALTED)"
    exit 1
fi

content.content:
Runs previously created tests and compares results against baselines.

Each matching test's command is re-executed, and the new output is compared
against the stored baseline. Any differences are recorded as potential regressions.

VERBOSITY LEVELS:
  -q      - Quiet: only exit code (0=pass, 1=fail)
  (none)  - Summary line with pass/fail counts and failing test names
  -v      - Also show failure details (difference counts and types)
  -vv     - Also show full diffs for each failure

EXAMPLES:
  reg-rs run -p my_test                    # run a specific test
  reg-rs run -p test                       # run all matching tests
  reg-rs run -p test -v                    # run with failure details
  reg-rs run -p test -vv                   # run with full diffs
  reg-rs run -q -p test                    # silent, check exit code only
  reg-rs r -p my_test -n                   # dry-run (show what would run)
  reg-rs r -p test --parallel              # run all matching tests in parallel

Usage: reg-rs run [OPTIONS]

Options:

          [default: .tdb]

content.subject: Insert new agentrail step for array-size-expressions
content.description: Use `agentrail insert --after 11` to slot array-size-expressions ahead of compound-literals (012). Step preempts cursor.

content.subject: Bootstrap feat/array-size-expressions branch
content.description: Create local dev (origin/dev doesn't exist yet), then create feat/array-size-expressions from it.

content.subject: Investigate current array-size parser in tc24r
content.description: Find where array bracket sizes are parsed; locate any existing const-expr evaluator (e.g. for enum values, sizeof, initializers); decide reuse vs minimal const-expr parser.

content.subject: Add positive and negative tests
content.description: Positive: `[A*B]`, `[N+1]`, `[(A+B)*C]`, `[N*16]`, `[A*(B+C)]`. Compare codegen vs literal-sized equivalents. Negative: runtime variable inside `[]`, broken expression `[A*]`. Regression: existing literal/single-identifier sizes still pass.

content.subject: Run full test suites and update docs
content.description: scripts/run-subset-tests.sh, scripts/run-chibicc-tests.sh, scripts/run-beej-tests.sh, reg-rs run -p tc24r. Update README.md noting supported expression forms in array sizes.

content.subject: Commit, rename feat→pr, agentrail complete
content.description: Commit on feat/array-size-expressions, rename branch to pr/array-size-expressions, then run agentrail complete with summary. DO NOT push (devgroup policy: coordinator relays).

content.subject: Improve const-expr error message to name offending identifier
content.description: Brief asks negative cases to name the offender ("must be a compile-time integer constant"). Current `const_primary` errors generically with "expected integer constant in array size". Add an Ident arm that produces a clearer message naming the token. Small, optional, but matches the brief.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# tc24r -- Tiny C Compiler for the COR24 FPGA Soft CPU

tc24r is an open-source C compiler targeting the **COR24** (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit) instruction set architecture. COR24 is an FPGA soft CPU designed by [MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) for efficient C code execution. The existing MakerLisp C compiler (cc24) is proprietary -- tc24r provides an open-source alternative.

The approach is inspired by [chibicc](https://github.com/rui314/chibicc), a small educational C compiler. tc24r follows the same architecture pattern (recursive-descent parser, direct assembly emission) but is written from scratch in Rust.

## Project Status

tc24r is **functional** -- it compiles real C programs to COR24 assembly that runs on hardware and the cor24-rs emulator. 14 components, ~50 crates, 61 demos all passing. See [docs/status.md](docs/status.md) for detailed status and test counts.

### What Works

- Types: int (24-bit), unsigned int, char (8-bit), void, pointers, arrays
- All standard C operators with correct precedence
- Control flow: if/else, while, do...while, for, break, continue, switch/case
- Ternary operator (? :), character literals, multi-declaration
- Prefix/postfix increment and decrement (++i, i--)
- Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>=
- sizeof, typedef, enum, struct (dot and arrow access), union
- Function pointers: local/global declarations, arrays, parameter passing, indirect calls
- Constant expressions in array sizes (int buf[ROWS * COLS])
- Function prototypes (forward declarations, mutual recursion)
- Functions with multiple parameters, recursion, ISR support
- Globals, string constants, hex literals
- Pointer arithmetic with element-size scaling
- Preprocessor: #define, #include, #pragma once
- Inline assembly: asm("...")
- MMIO: LED, UART TX/RX, interrupt enable
- Software division and modulo runtime

### What Does Not Work Yet

- Multi-file compilation
- float/double (COR24 has no FPU -- out of scope)

## COR24 Architecture at a Glance

- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
- **8 registers**: r0-r2 (general purpose), fp, sp, z (zero), iv, ir
- **32 instructions**: arithmetic, logic, shift, compare, branch, load/store, stack ops
- **1/2/4-byte** variable-length instruction encoding
- Hardware multiply (24-cycle), no hardware divide
- Little-endian, byte-addressable memory
- UART and GPIO via memory-mapped I/O

## Component Structure

The compiler is organized into 14 components with ~50 crates, each component
being its own Cargo workspace under `components/`:

| Component | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| core | Shared types: AST, tokens, spans, errors, traits |
| frontend | Preprocessing, lexing, parsing |
| backend | Legacy codegen and validation |
| codegen-emit | Assembly emission (core, data, load/store) |
| codegen-expr | Expression codegen (call, literal, ops, pointer, variable) |
| codegen-ops | Operator codegen (arithmetic, bitwise, compare, divmod, incdec, logical, unary, type-infer) |
| codegen-state | Codegen state management |
| codegen-stmt | Statement codegen (control flow, simple statements) |
| codegen-structure | Function structure (ISR, locals, prologue) |
| config | Configuration and target definition |
| dispatch | Codegen dispatch layer |
| macros | Proc macros (asm-dsl, emit-macros, handler-macros) |
| testing | Test harnesses (as24, compile, cor24, golden) |
| cli | CLI binary entry point |

See [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) for data flow and design constraints.

## Building

Build all components:

```bash
scripts/build-all.sh
```

This runs each component's `scripts/build.sh` in dependency order. The release binary is produced at:

```
components/cli/target/release/tc24r
```

To build a single component:

```bash
cargo build --manifest-path components/<name>/Cargo.toml
```

## Usage

```bash
tc24r <input.c> [-o output.s] [-I dir]
```

- `<input.c>` -- C source file to compile
- `-o output.s` -- output assembly file (default: stdout)
- `-I dir` -- add include search path (repeatable)

### Full Workflow

```bash
# Compile C to COR24 assembly
tc24r program.c -o program.s

# Assemble (requires as24 from COR24-TB archive)
as24 < program.s | longlgo > program.lgo

# Run on emulator (sw-cor24-emulator)
cd ~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator
cargo run -- run path/to/program.s
```

## Testing

| Test Suite | Pass | In-Scope | Coverage | Notes |
|-----------|------|----------|----------|-------|
| reg-rs regressions | 81 | 81 | 100% | Output stability (demos + chibicc + suites) |
| tc24r demos | 61 | 61 | 100% | End-to-end compiler + emulator |
| chibicc | 19 | 36 | 53% | 5 of 41 excluded: float, atomic, tls, varargs, unicode |
| beej-c-guide | 6 | 6 | 100% | 5 of 11 excluded: float/double (no FPU) |
| bgc examples | 41 | ~117* | ~35% | *Not testable on this machine; float exclusion TBD |

See [Testing Status](docs/testing-status.md) for detailed tables, blocker analysis, and progress charts.

```bash
demos/run-demo.sh              # run a single demo
scripts/run-subset-tests.sh    # run chibicc-subset tests
scripts/run-chibicc-tests.sh   # run full chibicc tests
```

## Documentation

| Document | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| [Language Reference](docs/reference.md) | All supported C features with examples |
| [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) | Component layout, data flow, design constraints |
| [Project Status](docs/status.md) | Current progress, test counts, demo list |
| [Future Plan](docs/plan.md) | Planned features and improvements |
| [ISA Summary](docs/isa-summary.md) | COR24 instruction set reference |
| [ABI Proposal](docs/abi-proposal.md) | Calling convention, stack frame layout |
| [Memory Map](docs/memory-map.md) | Address space, MMIO ports, stack location |
| [Assembler Syntax](docs/assembler-syntax.md) | as24 assembly syntax |
| [C Data Model](docs/c-data-model.md) | Type sizes and promotions |
| [Interrupt Plan](docs/interrupt-plan.md) | ISR support design |

### Development

| Document | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| [AI Agent Instructions](docs/ai_agent_instructions.md) | Guidelines for AI coding agents |
| [Development Process](docs/process.md) | TDD workflow and commit conventions |
| [Tools](docs/tools.md) | Recommended development tools |

## Tests

Tests across all components:

```bash
# Run all tests (build-all.sh runs tests too)
scripts/build-all.sh

# Run tests for one component
cargo test --manifest-path components/frontend/Cargo.toml

# Run ignored as24 validation tests (requires service on localhost:7412)
cargo test --manifest-path components/backend/Cargo.toml -- --ignored
```

## Projects Using This Compiler

The following COR24 ecosystem projects are written in C and built with `tc24r`:

- [sw-cor24-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-assembler) -- Native COR24 assembler (planned)
- [sw-cor24-macrolisp](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-macrolisp) -- Tiny Macro Lisp interpreter
- [sw-cor24-apl](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-apl) -- APL interpreter
- [sw-cor24-fortran](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-fortran) -- Fortran compiler (planned)
- [sw-cor24-script](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-script) -- SWS scripting language (Tcl-like)
- [sw-cor24-monitor](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-monitor) -- Resident monitor / service processor
- [sw-cor24-yocto-ed](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-yocto-ed) -- Tiny ed-style line editor

## License

MIT -- see [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.

Copyright (c) 2026 Michael A Wright

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(parser): const-expr in array sizes — tests, demo62, clearer error

The parser-side cascade for `+ - * /`, parens, and unary minus in array
sizes was already added in c641f9d and is exercised by demo55 and
existing tests, but two gaps remained:

- No coverage for parens/grouping or identifier×literal mixes beyond
  the basic `[ROWS * COLS]` shape demo55 hits.
- Error path for non-evaluable identifiers in `[ ... ]` was a generic
  "expected integer constant in array size" that didn't name the
  offender, making PL/SW-style failures hard to diagnose.

Changes:

- decl.rs: extend `const_primary` to recognize an identifier in array-
  size position and return a dedicated error naming it ("'FOO' must be
  a compile-time integer constant in array size"). Other malformed
  primaries keep the generic message.
- tc24r-parser-tests: 14 new tests covering literal mul/add/sub/div,
  paren grouping, nested parens, precedence (* tighter than +),
  left-associativity (/), unary minus in subexpr, named-identifier
  error message, broken expression, and a literal-size regression. Add
  a `try_parse_source` helper alongside the existing panicking one.
- demos/demo62.{c,sh} + work/reg-rs/tc24r-demo62.{rgt,out}: end-to-end
  lock-in for `[(A+B)*C]`, `[N*16]`, `[((A+1)*(B-1))]`, and `[A+B*C]`
  precedence.
- README.md: list the supported operator/parens forms and explicitly
  note that `sizeof` and VLAs are out of scope in this position.

Also retarget cor24-isa path-dep from sw-cor24-emulator/isa to the new
sw-cor24-isa repo (Phase 2 path migration, folded in to avoid keeping
a stale emulator sibling clone).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

  if git rev-parse --verify --quiet origin/dev >/dev/null \
     && git merge-base --is-ancestor "$pr" origin/dev 2>/dev/null; then
    git branch -D "$pr"
    echo "reaped:  $pr"
    reaped=$((reaped+1))
  else
    echo "pending: $pr (not yet in origin/dev)"
    pending=$((pending+1))
  fi
done < <(git for-each-ref --format='%(refname:short)' refs/heads/pr refs/heads/pr/\*)

echo
echo "Summary: ${reaped} reaped, ${pending} still pending."

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree >/dev/null
git fetch origin --prune

if git rev-parse --verify --quiet origin/dev >/dev/null; then
  git switch dev 2>/dev/null || git switch -c dev --track origin/dev
  git branch --set-upstream-to=origin/dev dev >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
  git merge --ff-only origin/dev
else
  echo "WARN: origin/dev does not exist yet; staying on current branch" >&2
fi

reaped=0 pending=0
while IFS= read -r pr; do
  [[ -z "$pr" ]] && continue
  if git rev-parse --verify --quiet origin/dev >/dev/null \
     && git merge-base --is-ancestor "$pr" origin/dev 2>/dev/null; then
    git branch -D "$pr"
    echo "reaped:  $pr"
    reaped=$((reaped+1))
  else
    echo "pending: $pr (not yet in origin/dev)"
    pending=$((pending+1))
  fi
done < <(git for-each-ref --format='%(refname:short)' refs/heads/pr refs/heads/pr/\*)

echo
echo "Summary: ${reaped} reaped, ${pending} still pending."
